recipes.title: Slow-Braised Beef & Root Vegetable Stew
recipes.desc: Fork-tender beef and sweet winter roots in a glossy red-wine gravy.
recipes.lede: This is the pot I put on when the afternoon turns grey. Brown the beef properly, let it go low and slow, and the gravy turns deep and glossy on its own.

steps.title: Dry & dredge
steps.text: Pat the beef very dry, season well, and toss in the flour until lightly coated.

steps.title: Sear in batches
steps.text: Heat the oil and brown the beef hard on all sides, in batches so it colours rather than steams. Set aside.

steps.title: Build the base
steps.text: Soften the onion in the same pot, add garlic and tomato paste, and cook a minute until it smells sweet and jammy.

steps.title: Deglaze
steps.text: Pour in the wine, scraping up every brown bit from the bottom, and let it reduce by half.

steps.title: Braise low
steps.text: Return the beef, add stock, bay, and thyme, cover, and simmer gently for 2 hours until the beef is nearly tender.

steps.title: Add the roots
steps.text: Stir in carrots and parsnips and cook uncovered 30–40 minutes more, until everything is soft and the gravy coats a spoon.

tips: Brown deeply — that dark crust is where the whole flavour of the gravy comes from.
No wine? Use all stock plus a spoon of balsamic for depth.
Even better the next day; the flavour rounds out overnight.

recipes.title: No-Knead Rustic Sourdough Loaf
recipes.desc: A crackly crust and open, tangy crumb from a fuss-free overnight rise.
recipes.lede: You don't need to knead and you don't need to hover. Time does the work here — mix in the evening, bake to a deep crust the next day.

steps.title: Mix the dough
steps.text: Stir starter into the warm water, then mix in the flour and salt until no dry patches remain. It will be shaggy.

steps.title: Fold, don't knead
steps.text: Over the next 2 hours, do four sets of stretch-and-folds, 30 minutes apart, then cover.

steps.title: Long rise
steps.text: Let it rise at room temperature until roughly doubled and airy, about 8–12 hours.

steps.title: Shape
steps.text: Tip out, shape into a tight round, and rest seam-up in a floured banneton for 1 hour.

steps.title: Preheat hot
steps.text: Heat a Dutch oven at 475°F / 245°C for 30 minutes so it's screaming hot.

steps.title: Score & bake
steps.text: Score the top, bake covered 25 minutes, then uncovered 18–20 minutes until deeply golden. Cool fully before slicing.

tips: Your starter should be bubbly and float in water before you begin.
Cool completely — cutting warm bread makes the crumb gummy.
A cast-iron pot traps steam for that bakery crust.

recipes.title: Summer Tomato & Basil Galette
recipes.desc: A free-form tart with a buttery crust, ripe tomatoes and melting cheese.
recipes.lede: The lazy cook's tart — no tin, no fluting, just a rustic fold of pastry around the best tomatoes you can find. Salt them first so the crust stays crisp.

steps.title: Make the pastry
steps.text: Rub butter into the flour with a pinch of salt until crumbly, add ice water, and bring together. Chill 30 minutes.

steps.title: Salt the tomatoes
steps.text: Lay the slices on paper towels, salt lightly, and leave 15 minutes to draw out water. Pat dry.

steps.title: Roll & fill
steps.text: Roll pastry into a rough circle, spread ricotta and parmesan in the centre, leaving a 2-inch border.

steps.title: Arrange
steps.text: Fan the tomato slices over the cheese and tuck torn basil between them.

steps.title: Fold & glaze
steps.text: Fold the border up over the edge, pleating as you go, and brush the pastry with beaten egg.

steps.title: Bake
steps.text: Bake at 400°F / 200°C for 38–42 minutes until the crust is deep gold. Finish with more basil.

tips: Salting the tomatoes is the difference between crisp and soggy — don't skip it.
Keep the butter cold; warm pastry loses its flake.
Great warm or at room temperature for a picnic.

recipes.title: Herb-Roasted Garlic Chicken
recipes.desc: Crisp-skinned roast chicken with lemon, garlic and a pan of soft herbs.
recipes.lede: The Sunday anchor. Butter under the skin, a lemon in the cavity, and a hot oven — that's the whole trick to juicy meat and shatter-crisp skin.

steps.title: Dry the bird
steps.text: Pat the chicken completely dry, inside and out — dry skin is crisp skin.

steps.title: Herb butter
steps.text: Mash butter with garlic, chopped herbs, and salt, then push most of it under the breast skin.

steps.title: Season & stuff
steps.text: Rub the outside with oil and the rest of the butter, and tuck the lemon halves inside.

steps.title: Truss loosely
steps.text: Tie the legs together and tuck the wing tips under so it roasts evenly.

steps.title: Roast hot
steps.text: Roast at 425°F / 220°C for about 1 hour 15 minutes, until the thigh juices run clear (74°C internal).

steps.title: Rest
steps.text: Rest 15 minutes before carving so the juices settle back into the meat.

tips: Let the chicken sit uncovered in the fridge a few hours first for even crispier skin.
Save the carcass — it makes the beef stew's cousin, a golden stock.
No thermometer? Juices at the thigh joint should run clear, not pink.

recipes.title: Baked Oats with Pear & Honey
recipes.desc: Warm, custardy baked oats with soft pears and a drizzle of honey.
recipes.lede: Somewhere between porridge and cake. Assemble it half-asleep, slide it in the oven, and breakfast makes itself while the coffee brews.

steps.title: Heat the oven
steps.text: Preheat to 350°F / 180°C and butter a small baking dish.

steps.title: Whisk the custard
steps.text: Whisk milk, egg, honey, vanilla, cinnamon, and baking powder until smooth.

steps.title: Combine
steps.text: Stir in the oats and half the pears, then pour into the dish.

steps.title: Top
steps.text: Fan the remaining pear slices over the surface and drizzle with a little extra honey.

steps.title: Bake
steps.text: Bake 32–36 minutes until set in the middle and golden at the edges. Rest 5 minutes before spooning out.

tips: Swap pears for apple or berries with the seasons.
A spoon of yoghurt on top turns it into pudding.
Leftovers reheat beautifully — make a double batch.

recipes.title: Buttermilk Skillet Cornbread
recipes.desc: Golden, tender cornbread with crisp edges from a screaming-hot skillet.
recipes.lede: Poured into a hot, buttered skillet so the edges fry crisp while the middle stays soft. Barely sweet — the way it's meant to go beside a bowl of stew.

steps.title: Heat the skillet
steps.text: Put a cast-iron skillet in the oven and preheat both to 425°F / 220°C.

steps.title: Dry then wet
steps.text: Whisk the dry ingredients in one bowl; whisk buttermilk, eggs, honey, and most of the butter in another.

steps.title: Combine gently
steps.text: Fold wet into dry until just barely mixed — a few lumps are fine, overmixing makes it tough.

steps.title: Sizzle
steps.text: Carefully swirl the last of the butter into the hot skillet, then pour in the batter — it should hiss.

steps.title: Bake
steps.text: Bake 22–25 minutes until golden and a skewer comes out clean. Serve warm, straight from the pan.

tips: The hot skillet is everything — that's what crisps the edge.
No buttermilk? Stir a spoon of lemon juice into regular milk and wait 5 minutes.
Add a diced chilli or a handful of corn for a savoury twist.

recipes.title: Lemon & Olive Oil Cake
recipes.desc: A moist, fragrant cake with bright lemon and a soft olive-oil crumb.
recipes.lede: Olive oil keeps this cake moist for days and lets the lemon sing. One bowl, a whisk, and no mixer required.

steps.title: Prep the tin
steps.text: Preheat to 350°F / 180°C and line a 9-inch round tin.

steps.title: Whisk the wet
steps.text: Whisk sugar, eggs, and lemon zest until pale, then stream in the olive oil, milk, and lemon juice.

steps.title: Fold the dry
steps.text: Sift in flour, baking powder, and salt, and fold until just combined.

steps.title: Bake
steps.text: Pour in and bake 42–46 minutes until golden and a skewer comes out clean.

steps.title: Finish
steps.text: Cool in the tin 10 minutes, then dust with sugar or brush with a little warmed honey.

tips: Use a fruity olive oil you'd happily eat — it's the headline flavour.
Zest before you juice; it's far easier that way.
Keeps moist under a cloche for 3–4 days.

recipes.title: Wild Blackberry Jam
recipes.desc: A small-batch jam with deep berry flavour and a soft, spoonable set.
recipes.lede: Made the old way with just fruit, sugar, and lemon — no pectin sachets. Small batches set best and keep the flavour bright and fresh.

steps.title: Chill a plate
steps.text: Put a small plate in the freezer — you'll use it to test the set.

steps.title: Macerate
steps.text: Toss the berries with sugar and lemon juice and let them sit 15 minutes until juicy.

steps.title: Cook down
steps.text: Bring to a boil with the water, then simmer, stirring often, for about 20 minutes until thick and glossy.

steps.title: Test the set
steps.text: Drop a little on the cold plate; if it wrinkles when pushed, it's ready. If not, cook a few minutes more.

steps.title: Jar it
steps.text: Skim any foam, ladle into clean warm jars, seal, and cool. Keeps 3 weeks in the fridge.

tips: Under-ripe berries carry more natural pectin and help the set.
Keep the batch small — big pots of jam take forever and dull the flavour.
For a seedless jam, press half the fruit through a sieve first.

recipes.title: Sweet Red Bean Paste (Anko)
recipes.desc: Silky-sweet azuki bean paste — the soul of so many Japanese sweets.
recipes.lede: A little nod to the recipe that inspired this whole site. Just beans, sugar, and salt, cooked patiently until thick and glossy. Leave it chunky or blend it smooth.

steps.title: Rinse & parboil
steps.text: Rinse the beans, cover generously with water, bring to a boil, then drain to remove any bitterness.

steps.title: Simmer tender
steps.text: Cover with fresh water and simmer gently 60–90 minutes, topping up water so they stay submerged, until a bean mashes easily.

steps.title: Sweeten
steps.text: Drain, return to the pot over low heat, and stir in the sugar in two additions, then the salt.

steps.title: Cook to a paste
steps.text: Keep stirring until you can draw a line across the bottom of the pot with a spatula — it thickens more as it cools.

steps.title: Chunky or smooth
steps.text: Leave it as-is for chunky tsubuan, or blend to a fine paste for smooth koshian. Spread on a tray to cool.

tips: Modern beans don't need overnight soaking — start straight away.
A pinch of salt makes the sweetness taste rounder, not saltier.
Stop at the line test; carry-over heat firms it up as it cools.

recipes.title: Classic Buttermilk Pancakes
recipes.desc: Fluffy, tender pancakes with a golden griddle crust — the everyday stack.
recipes.lede: The pancake fundamentals, no fuss required. Whisk it loose, don't overwork it, and let a hot pan do the rest.

steps.title: Sift the dry ingredients
steps.text: Sift the flour, baking soda, salt, and sugar together into a bowl.

steps.title: Make a well
steps.text: Make a hole in the middle and crack in the egg.

steps.title: Mix quickly
steps.text: Break the yolk and pour in the buttermilk, mixing quickly until you have a sticky batter.

steps.title: Don't overwork it
steps.text: Stop as soon as it comes together — beating brings out the gluten in the flour, which stops the pancakes rising.

steps.title: Fry & serve
steps.text: Fry spoonfuls of batter in a hot griddle pan and serve right away, while they're still hot.

tips: A few lumps in the batter are exactly what you want — smooth means overmixed.
No buttermilk? Stir a spoon of lemon juice into regular milk and let it sit 5 minutes.
Wait for bubbles to cover the surface before you flip — that's the pan telling you it's ready.
